role_summary: SNCC field secretary; treasurer and SNCC representative of the Baltimore Civic Interest Group (CIG); attended the July 1961 founding SNCC coordinating committee meeting in Louisville as CIG's representative; fall 1961 voter-registration organizer in McComb, Mississippi alongside Bob Moses, helping establish the voter-registration school above the Burgland Supermarket; 1963 organizer in Cambridge, Maryland alongside Gloria Richardson.
ai_reading: Robinson's strongest semantic neighbor in this corpus is Charles McLaurin (entry 17), whose passages recur 61 times across Robinson's chunk neighborhoods and reach the single highest cosine score on the page (0.814). Both worked the early Mississippi voter-registration field, Robinson in McComb in fall 1961 and McLaurin in Sunflower County from 1962, and the embedding reads that shared field-secretary register as a tighter pairing than either man's link to the SNCC headquarters voices or to the later Freedom Summer cohort. The less expected signal sits one rung down: Gloria Hayes Richardson (entry 49) is Robinson's second-densest neighbor (count 42), and the influence graph runs an edge from her interview to his, meaning she names him on the record. Cosine similarity and the who-discusses-whom graph therefore both anchor Robinson to the Cambridge, Maryland campaign as firmly as to Mississippi, which cuts against the historiographical habit of filing him under the McComb project alone. The vocabulary that travels with him across Baltimore, McComb, and Cambridge reads to the embedding as one continuous organizer signature rather than three separate regional episodes.
biographical_paragraph: Robinson's LoC oral history carries a low audit tier after a Library of Congress cross-reference pass, so the routine caution applies that any specific claim quoted from the transcript should be confirmed against external sources before publication; the external biographical record is documented through the SNCC Digital Gateway and the institutional history of the Baltimore Civic Interest Group [src: 1, src: 2]. Robinson was born October 2, 1939 and raised in Baltimore, Maryland [src: 2]. He came into the Baltimore Civic Interest Group through Walter Dixon, the City Councilman who was also dean of his business college, became the group's treasurer, and attended the July 1961 SNCC coordinating committee meeting at Anne and Carl Braden's home in Louisville, Kentucky, where he met Ella Baker [src: 2]. In fall 1961 he traveled to McComb, Mississippi to work voter registration alongside Bob Moses, helping open the voter-registration school above the Burgland Supermarket; the project closed and SNCC staff withdrew that autumn after white violence escalated, including the September 25, 1961 murder of Amite County farmer Herbert Lee by state representative E. H. Hurst [src: 2, src: 4]. He returned to Maryland, where CIG's registration drive added close to 10,000 Black voters to the Baltimore rolls during his tenure, and in 1963 he joined the Cambridge, Maryland campaign alongside Gloria Hayes Richardson (entry 49), chair of the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee [src: 2, src: 5]. He inspired Northern-college SNCC recruits including Judy Richardson (entry 69), later a producer on the Eyes on the Prize documentary series [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Reginald Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Robinson recalls the instruction he was given in his first SNCC training about why field secretaries were not to settle into the towns they organized:
interview_snippets.quote: But you want to go in and build something and leave something, not to go and make a home there.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reginald Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: He restates the same organizing principle as a measure of success, tracing it back to Ella Baker:
interview_snippets.quote: So it was always important to know that what you're doing is you're building things for somebody else to build on.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reginald Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the constitutional-interpretation test the Mississippi registrar used to fail Black applicants in McComb:
interview_snippets.quote: he would take the Constitution and flip the pages and turn to any page in the book

interview_snippets.speaker: Reginald Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reporting on the Baltimore registration drive at the Louisville SNCC meeting led the others to assume an expertise he did not claim for himself:
interview_snippets.quote: They thought I was a voter registration expert.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
interview_snippets.lead_in: Gloria Hayes Richardson, chair of the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee, names the two SNCC organizers who came to Cambridge, Maryland after her cousin Frederick St. Clair urged them to stop there:
interview_snippets.quote: And then they took, yeah, they had Bill Hansen, Reginald Robinson.

movement_context: Robinson's fall 1961 McComb assignment placed him inside the first SNCC voter-registration project in the Mississippi Delta, the Pike County campaign Bob Moses had begun that July at the request of Amzie Moore and the NAACP's Mississippi State Conference [src: 3]. The voter-registration school above the Burgland Supermarket on Summit Street in McComb served as the operational base for the Pike, Walthall, and Amite County registration drives; SNCC field secretaries including Robinson, Moses, Charles McLaurin (catalog entry 17), Marion Barry, Travis Britt, and John Hardy worked from that location through September and October 1961, walking rural Black residents to the Pike County Courthouse to attempt to register [src: 3, 4]. The September 25, 1961 murder of Amite County dairy farmer Herbert Lee by Mississippi state representative E. H. Hurst (an act for which Hurst was never indicted, and which preceded the 1964 reprisal murder of witness Louis Allen) marked the operational closing of the first McComb project; SNCC field staff withdrew from McComb through October and November 1961 as white-supremacist violence escalated beyond what the small field-secretary contingent could continue to operate within [src: 4]. After McComb, Robinson returned to Maryland, where the Baltimore Civic Interest Group's voter-registration drive in the early 1960s registered close to 10,000 Black voters in Baltimore; the CIG was the Mid-Atlantic urban-North counterpart to the Mississippi Delta rural-South work, and Robinson's institutional position as treasurer and SNCC representative made him one of the principal cross-border organizing bridges between the two project geographies [src: 2]. In 1963 Robinson worked the Cambridge, Maryland campaign alongside Gloria Hayes Richardson (catalog entry 49); the campaign produced the July 1963 Treaty of Cambridge, which Richardson declined to endorse at the U.S. Department of Justice, and the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee Richardson chaired was the SNCC-affiliated organizing body on Maryland's Eastern Shore, which connected the field operation to the wider SNCC project-county network [src: 5]. In his oral history Robinson recounts marrying in 1966 or 1967, in a ceremony at Harry Belafonte's house with Ella Baker present, and describes his wife as a psychiatric social worker; the transcript does not give her name [src: 1].
legacy_and_reception: The SNCC Digital Gateway's biographical entry treats Robinson's later Baltimore organizing as a continuous extension of his McComb-and-Cambridge field-secretary trajectory rather than as a separate phase, and it is the principal continuously-maintained source for the arc of his life outside the LoC oral history itself [src: 2]. His McComb 1961 record sits inside the documentary stream that the standard scholarly histories of SNCC and of the Mississippi movement built out across the 1980s and 1990s: Clayborne Carson's In Struggle situates the fall 1961 Pike County project and the field-staff withdrawal after the Herbert Lee murder within SNCC's institutional history [src: 3], and John Dittmer's Local People reconstructs the same McComb sequence, including the September 25, 1961 killing of Herbert Lee and the 1964 reprisal murder of witness Louis Allen, from the vantage of Mississippi's local movement [src: 4]. The Howell Raines oral-history volume My Soul Is Rested, drawn from interviews conducted in the mid-1970s, preserves first-person testimony from McComb-project participants in a contemporaneous documentary register that predates those later scholarly accounts [src: 6]. Robinson's own contemporaneous correspondence and movement materials are among the SNCC-era holdings the SNCC Legacy Project and the Duke University documentary platform have gathered from the cohort of organizers active in the Baltimore Civic Interest Group, the McComb project, and the Cambridge campaign [src: 2].

gallery.alt: Bob Moses, 1962 University of Texas Cactus yearbook portrait
gallery.caption: Bob Moses in a 1961 portrait that appeared in the 1962 Cactus yearbook (University of Texas at Austin, page 344). Moses was the SNCC field secretary who directed the McComb, Mississippi voter-registration project that summer and fall of 1961; Reginald Robinson joined the McComb operation alongside Moses, helping run the voter-registration school above the Burgland Supermarket on Summit Street through October 1961.
gallery.photographer: Unknown (yearbook portrait; not credited in The Cactus, 1962, page 344)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from The Cactus, University of Texas at Austin yearbook, 1962)
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1977 without copyright notice or renewal)

gallery.alt: SNCC poster 'One Man, One Vote' (ca. 1963), photograph by Danny Lyon
gallery.caption: Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee poster 'One Man, One Vote' from circa 1963, photograph by Danny Lyon (SNCC staff photographer). The 'One Man, One Vote' slogan framed the SNCC voter-registration program Robinson worked in McComb in fall 1961 and in the Baltimore Civic Interest Group registration drive that, by his account in the SNCC Digital Gateway record, registered close to 10,000 Black voters in Baltimore during his tenure as CIG treasurer and SNCC representative. Held by the Seattle Public Library and contributed to the Digital Public Library of America.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Seattle Public Library and the Digital Public Library of America)
gallery.license: No Known Copyright (RightsStatements.org designation; the holding institution reasonably believes no copyright or related rights restrict use)

gallery.alt: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project recruitment broadside, April 1964
gallery.caption: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project recruitment broadside, April 1964, two and a half years after Robinson's fall 1961 McComb work. The Mississippi voter-registration project Robinson helped seed at the Burgland Supermarket school in 1961 became, by 1964, the institutional anchor for the Council of Federated Organizations Freedom Summer program that brought roughly one thousand Northern student volunteers into the Mississippi Delta. Held by the Mississippi Department of Archives and History.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Mississippi Department of Archives and History)

sources.title: Peter B. Levy, Civil War on Race Street: The Civil Rights Movement in Cambridge, Maryland
sources.publisher: University Press of Florida, 2003; WorldCat OCLC 51163879 (the standard scholarly institutional history of the Cambridge Movement, the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee, the SNCC field-secretary network including Robinson that worked the Cambridge campaign in 1963, and the Treaty of Cambridge negotiation)
